title: Ōmi Province

infobox.value: (近江国, *Ōmi no Kuni*; Japanese pronunciation: [[oꜜː.mʲi (no kɯ.ɲi)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1)) was a [province of Japan](/wiki/Provinces_of_Japan "Provinces of Japan"), which today comprises [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was one of the provinces that made up the [Tōsandō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dsand%C5%8D "Tōsandō") [circuit](/wiki/Circuit_(subnational_entity) "Circuit (subnational entity)"). Its nickname is **Gōshū** (江州). Under the *[Engishiki](/wiki/Engishiki "Engishiki")* classification system, Ōmi was ranked as one of the 13 "great countries" (大国) in terms of importance, and one of the "near countries" (近国) in terms of distance from the imperial capital Kyoto.

first_paragraph: "Ōmi" redirects here. For the city in current Shiga Prefecture, see [Ōmihachiman](/wiki/%C5%8Cmihachiman "Ōmihachiman"). For other uses, see [Omi (disambiguation)](/wiki/Omi_(disambiguation) "Omi (disambiguation)").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

"Ōmi" redirects here. For the city in current Shiga Prefecture, see [Ōmihachiman](/wiki/%C5%8Cmihachiman "Ōmihachiman"). For other uses, see [Omi (disambiguation)](/wiki/Omi_(disambiguation) "Omi (disambiguation)").

Former province of Japan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Provinces_of_Japan-Omi.svg/330px-Provinces_of_Japan-Omi.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Provinces_of_Japan-Omi.svg)

Map of Japanese provinces (1868) with Omi Province highlighted

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Hiroshige_Boats_on_a_lake.jpg/330px-Hiroshige_Boats_on_a_lake.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiroshige_Boats_on_a_lake.jpg)

[Ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") print by [Hiroshige](/wiki/Hiroshige "Hiroshige") of the sailboats at Yahashi, one of the *[Eight Views of Ōmi](/wiki/Eight_Views_of_%C5%8Cmi "Eight Views of Ōmi")*, c. 1834

**Ōmi Province** (近江国, *Ōmi no Kuni*; Japanese pronunciation: [[oꜜː.mʲi (no kɯ.ɲi)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[1]](#cite_note-1)) was a [province of Japan](/wiki/Provinces_of_Japan "Provinces of Japan"), which today comprises [Shiga Prefecture](/wiki/Shiga_Prefecture "Shiga Prefecture").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was one of the provinces that made up the [Tōsandō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dsand%C5%8D "Tōsandō") [circuit](/wiki/Circuit_(subnational_entity) "Circuit (subnational entity)"). Its nickname is **Gōshū** (江州). Under the *[Engishiki](/wiki/Engishiki "Engishiki")* classification system, Ōmi was ranked as one of the 13 "great countries" (大国) in terms of importance, and one of the "near countries" (近国) in terms of distance from the imperial capital Kyoto.

Ōmi bordered on [Wakasa](/wiki/Wakasa_Province "Wakasa Province") and [Echizen Provinces](/wiki/Echizen_Province "Echizen Province") to the north, [Mino](/wiki/Mino_Province "Mino Province") and [Ise Provinces](/wiki/Ise_Province "Ise Province") to the east, [Iga](/wiki/Iga_Province "Iga Province") and [Yamato Provinces](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province") to the south, and [Yamashiro](/wiki/Yamashiro_Province "Yamashiro Province") and [Tanba Provinces](/wiki/Tanba_Province "Tanba Province") to the west. [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa"), Japan's largest lake, is located at the center of the province.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi_Province&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The area of Ōmi has been settled since at least the [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period"), and the traces of several large settlements have been found. During the [Kofun period](/wiki/Kofun_period "Kofun period"), the area appears to have been dominated by several powerful immigrant clans, most notably the [Wani clan](/wiki/Wani_(scholar) "Wani (scholar)"), which was originally from [Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje"), a state in the southwestern Korean Peninsula. The names Ōmi and Lake Biwa do not appear in the *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")*, *[Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū")* or other ancient documents.

Ōmi initially had various names. Wooden tags in archaic Japanese from the ruins of [Asuka, Yamato](/wiki/Asuka,_Yamato "Asuka, Yamato"), the [Yamato Kingship](/wiki/Yamato_Kingship "Yamato Kingship")'s capital on and off from 538-710, mention the Awapumi (淡海, *Clear Lake*), or variations thereof, including Tikaumi (近淡海, *Freshwater Lake*). The archaic name *Apapumi* evolved into the [current Japanese form](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), Ōmi no umi (近江海, *Sea of Freshwater*). The name was fixed as [Old Japanese](/wiki/Old_Japanese "Old Japanese") Awafumi (淡海) only after the enactment and enforcement of the [Taiho Code](/wiki/Taiho_Code "Taiho Code") in 701 and the decree of 713, which stipulated that the names of the provinces defined under the [Ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō") system should be written using two auspicious [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji").

The Yamato state ruled briefly from this region. In the late [Asuka period](/wiki/Asuka_period "Asuka period") (538-710), [Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Emperor_Tenji "Emperor Tenji") (r. 626 – January 7, 672) built [Ōmi Ōtsu Palace](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_%C5%8Ctsu_Palace "Ōmi Ōtsu Palace") in what is now the city of [Ōtsu](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu "Ōtsu") and ruled from it from 667 until 672. He issued the first legal code, the [Ōmi Code](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Code "Ōmi Code"), while ruling from the Ōmi Ōtsu Palace. After his death, the [Jinshin War](/wiki/Jinshin_War "Jinshin War") broke out among his fourteen children, one of the largest conflicts in Japanese history. Ōmi was the site of several battles, and afterwards, the capital returned to Asuka.

In the subsequent [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period"), during the reign of [Emperor Shōmu](/wiki/Emperor_Sh%C5%8Dmu "Emperor Shōmu") (September 22, 701 – June 4, 756), the new [Ritsuryō](/wiki/Ritsury%C5%8D "Ritsuryō") system established a [kokufu](/wiki/Kokufu "Kokufu") (provincial capital) at [Ōmi Kokuchō](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Kokuch%C5%8D "Ōmi Kokuchō") in Ōtsu, near the ruins of the former Ōmi Ōtsu Palace. He also established a [provincial temple](/wiki/Provincial_temple "Provincial temple") there. In 740, Emperor Shōmu built a personal residence, called *Rakumura*, in what is now [Kōka, Shiga](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka,_Shiga "Kōka, Shiga"). He declared it the new capital in 745 and renamed it [Shigaraki Palace](/wiki/Shigaraki_Palace "Shigaraki Palace"), but abandoned it the same year due to expenses.

[Takebe taisha](/wiki/Takebe_taisha "Takebe taisha"), enshrining [Yamato Takeru](/wiki/Yamato_Takeru "Yamato Takeru") and his relics and [Ōkuninushi](/wiki/%C5%8Ckuninushi "Ōkuninushi"), was designated as the [ichinomiya](/wiki/Ichinomiya "Ichinomiya") (chief shrine) for the province.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

During the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794-1195), the proximity of Ōmi to the capital at [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō"), its location on the [Tōkaidō](/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_(road) "Tōkaidō (road)") and [Nakasendō](/wiki/Nakasend%C5%8D "Nakasendō") highways connecting the capital with eastern Japan, and the main route from the capital to the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan"), all gave the province great strategic importance. With the spread of [Buddhism in Japan](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan"), [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") founded the great [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") monastery of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") in 788 on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") in Ōmi.

From the late Heian into the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period"), the [Sasaki clan](/wiki/Sasaki_clan "Sasaki clan") controlled the post of [shugo](/wiki/Shugo "Shugo") (military governor) of Ōmi Province, and two [cadet branches](/wiki/Cadet_branch "Cadet branch"), the [Rokkaku](/wiki/Rokkaku_clan "Rokkaku clan") and [Kyōgoku](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Dgoku_clan "Kyōgoku clan"), dominated the province into the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period").

In the tumultuous [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), internal struggles weakened both clans. Northern Ōmi became a battleground between the [Azai](/wiki/Azai_clan "Azai clan") and [Asakura](/wiki/Asakura_clan "Asakura clan") clans. In the south, the Rokkaku were supported by the [Kōka ikki](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka_ikki "Kōka ikki"), whose [shinobi](/wiki/Ninja "Ninja") operatives were infamous. In the late 1560s and early 1570s, [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") invaded from the east, defeating the Azai, Asakura, Rokkaku, Kōka, and finally, in 1573, the [Ashikaga shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate") itself. Nobunaga built [Azuchi Castle](/wiki/Azuchi_Castle "Azuchi Castle") near the southwestern side of Lake Biwa in Ōmi, from which he planned to rule all of Japan and beyond.

After [Nobunaga was assassinated at Honnō-ji](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident") on 21 June 1582, [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") awarded much of the province to [Ishida Mitsunari](/wiki/Ishida_Mitsunari "Ishida Mitsunari"). Mitsunari would later be [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu")'s archrival at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") on October 21, 1600.

After the establishment of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") in 1603, much of the province was divided into several [feudal domains](/wiki/Han_system "Han system"), the largest of which was [Hikone Domain](/wiki/Hikone_Domain "Hikone Domain"), ruled by the [Ii clan](/wiki/Ii_clan "Ii clan"). Ōmi continued to serve as a transportation conduit, with five of the [53 Stations of the Tōkaidō](/wiki/53_Stations_of_the_T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D "53 Stations of the Tōkaidō") and eight of the [69 Stations of the Nakasendō](/wiki/69_Stations_of_the_Nakasend%C5%8D "69 Stations of the Nakasendō").

Following the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration"), on November 22, 1871, Ōtsu Prefecture and Nagahama Prefecture were created from former [tenryō](/wiki/Tenry%C5%8D "Tenryō") and [hatamoto](/wiki/Hatamoto "Hatamoto") territories within the province, and each former domain formed its own prefecture. These were merged on January 19, 1872 to form Shiga Prefecture. From August 21, 1876 to February 7, 1881, the Reinan region of [Fukui Prefecture](/wiki/Fukui_Prefecture "Fukui Prefecture") (west of [Tsuruga, Fukui](/wiki/Tsuruga,_Fukui "Tsuruga, Fukui")) was part of Shiga Prefecture, thus giving it a shoreline on the [Sea of Japan](/wiki/Sea_of_Japan "Sea of Japan"). Local inhabitants strongly opposed the merger, and it was withdrawn.

## Historical districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi_Province&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historical districts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b8/Districts_of_Omi_province.png)](/wiki/File:Districts_of_Omi_province.png)

Ōmi was divided into 12 Districts (郡), which were further subdivided into 93 counties (郷), containing 1,597 villages. The total assessed value of the province in terms of *[kokudaka](/wiki/Kokudaka "Kokudaka")* was 858,618 *[koku](/wiki/Koku "Koku")*.

* [Azai District](/wiki/Azai_District,_%C5%8Cmi "Azai District, Ōmi") (浅井郡)
  + [Higashiazai District](/wiki/Higashiazai_District,_Shiga "Higashiazai District, Shiga") (東浅井郡) – dissolved
  + [Nishiazai District](/wiki/Nishiazai_District,_Shiga "Nishiazai District, Shiga") (西浅井郡) – merged into Ika District on April 1, 1897
* [Echi District](/wiki/Echi_District,_Shiga "Echi District, Shiga") (愛知郡)
* [Gamō District](/wiki/Gam%C5%8D_District,_Shiga "Gamō District, Shiga") (蒲生郡)
* [Ika District](/wiki/Ika_District,_Shiga "Ika District, Shiga") (伊香郡) – absorbed Nishiazai District on April 1, 1897; now dissolved
* [Inukami District](/wiki/Inukami_District,_Shiga "Inukami District, Shiga") (犬上郡)
* [Kanzaki District](/wiki/Kanzaki_District,_Shiga "Kanzaki District, Shiga") (神崎郡) – dissolved
* [Kōka District](/wiki/K%C5%8Dka_District,_Shiga "Kōka District, Shiga") (甲賀郡) – dissolved
* [Kurita District](/wiki/Kurita_District,_Shiga "Kurita District, Shiga") (栗太郡) – dissolved
* [Sakata District](/wiki/Sakata_District,_Shiga "Sakata District, Shiga") (坂田郡) – dissolved
* [Shiga District](/wiki/Shiga_District,_Shiga "Shiga District, Shiga") (滋賀郡) – dissolved
* [Takashima District](/wiki/Takashima_District,_Shiga "Takashima District, Shiga") (高島郡) – dissolved
* [Yasu District](/wiki/Yasu_District,_Shiga "Yasu District, Shiga") (野洲郡) – dissolved

## Edo-period Domains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi_Province&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Edo-period Domains")]

List of the domains of Ōmi Province

| Name | Capital | Ruling clan and kokudaka |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Hikone Domain](/wiki/Hikone_Domain "Hikone Domain") | [Sawayama Castle](/wiki/Sawayama_Castle "Sawayama Castle")  (1600- 1606) [Hikone Castle](/wiki/Hikone_Castle "Hikone Castle")  (1606- 1871) | Ii clan (1600 - 1871, 180,000→150,000→200,000→250,000→300,000 (350,000→200,000→230,000) |
| [Zeze Domain](/wiki/Zeze_Domain "Zeze Domain") | [Zeze Castle](/wiki/Zeze_Castle "Zeze Castle") | Toda clan (1601 - 1616, 30,000 koku)  Honda clan (1616 - 1621, 30,000 koku)  Suganuma clan (1621-1634, 31,000 koku)  Ishikawa clan (1634 - 1651, 70,000→ 53,000 koku)  Honda clan (1651 - 1871 , 70,000 koku) |
| [Minakuchi Domain](/wiki/Minakuchi_Domain "Minakuchi Domain") | [Minakuchi Castle](/wiki/Minakuchi_Castle "Minakuchi Castle") | Kato clan (1682 - 1695, 20,000 koku)  Torii clan (1695 - 1712, 20,000 koku)  Kato clan (1712 - 1871, 25,000 koku) |
| [Ōmizo Domain](/wiki/%C5%8Cmizo_Domain "Ōmizo Domain") | Ōmizo jin'ya | Wakebe clan (1619- 1871, 20,000 koku) |
| [Nisshōji Domain](/wiki/Nissh%C5%8Dji_Domain "Nisshōji Domain") | Nisshōji jin'ya | Ichihashi clan (1620 - 1871, 20,000→18,000→17,000 koku) |
| [Yamakami Domain](/wiki/Yamakami_Domain "Yamakami Domain") | Yamakami jin'ya | Ando clan (1604 - 1695, 10,000 koku)  Inagaki clan, (1698 - 1871, 13,000 koku) |
| [Ōmi-Miyagawa Domain](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi-Miyagawa_Domain "Ōmi-Miyagawa Domain") | Miyagawa jin'ya | Hotta clan (1698 - 1871, 10,000→13,000) |
| [Mikami Domain](/wiki/Mikami_Domain "Mikami Domain") | Mikami jinya | Endo clan (1698 - 1870, 10,000→12,000 koku) |
| [Katata Domain](/w/index.php?title=Katata_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Katata Domain (page does not exist)") | Katata jin'ya | Hotta clan (1698 - 1826, 10,000→13,000 koku)  Transferred to Shimotsuke [Sano Domain](/wiki/Sano_Domain "Sano Domain") |
| [Kutsuki Domain](/w/index.php?title=Kutsuki_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kutsuki Domain (page does not exist)") | Kutsuki jin'ya | Kutsuki clan (1636 - 1648, 10,000 koku)  transferred to Shimotsuke [Kanuma Domain](/w/index.php?title=Kanuma_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kanuma Domain (page does not exist)") |
| [Ōmori Domain](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmori_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōmori Domain (page does not exist)") | Ōmori jin'ya | Mogami clan (1622 - 1632, 10,000 koku)  attainder |
| [Ōmi-Takashima Domain](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi-Takashima_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōmi-Takashima Domain (page does not exist)") |  | Sakuma clan (1600 - 1616, 15,000→20,000 koku)  transfer to Shinano [Iiyama domain](/w/index.php?title=Iiyama_domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Iiyama domain (page does not exist)"), later attainder |
| [Ōmi-Komuro Domain](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cmi-Komuro_Domain&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōmi-Komuro Domain (page does not exist)") | Komuro jin'ya | Kobori clan (1619 -1788, 12,460→11,460→16,300 koku)  attainder due to mismanagement |
